question: Gram positive cells?

options.feedback: Incorrect. While this is a characteristic of Gram-positive cells, it is not the only one.

options.text: have large amounts of teichoic acids.
options.feedback: Incorrect. While this is a characteristic of Gram-positive cells, it is not the only one.

options.text: do not have an outer membrane.
options.feedback: Incorrect. While this is a characteristic of Gram-positive cells, it is not the only one.

options.text: all of the above are true.
options.feedback: Correct! Gram-positive cells have thick cell walls, teichoic acids, and lack an outer membrane.

question: Which of the following agent is used to prevent Malaria

options.feedback: Incorrect. Mebendazole is an anti-helminthic drug.
Correct! Chloroquine is used for malaria prophylaxis and treatment.
Incorrect. There is no licensed vaccine for malaria currently in widespread use.
Incorrect. Zinc is a mineral supplement, not an anti-malarial.

question: Each of the following statements concerning Ascaris lumbricoides is correct except:

options.text: Ascaris lumbricoides is one of the largest nematode
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a correct statement.
Incorrect. This is a correct statement.

options.text: Both dogs and cats are intermediate host of Ascaris lumbricoides
options.feedback: Correct! Humans are the definitive host; there is no intermediate host for A. lumbricoides.

options.text: A lumbricoides is transmitted by ingestion of eggs
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a correct statement.

question: Each of the following statements concerning kala-azar is correct except:

options.text: Kala-azar is caused by leishmania donovani
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a correct statement.

options.text: kala-azar is transmitted by the bite of sandflies
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a correct statement.

options.text: Kala-azar occurs primarily in rural latin America
options.feedback: Correct! Kala-azar (visceral leishmaniasis) primarily occurs in the Indian subcontinent, East Africa, and Brazil, but saying it occurs primarily in rural Latin America is an overstatement and not accurate.

options.text: Kala-azar can be diagnosed by finding amastigotes in bone marrow
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a correct statement.

question: Each of the following statements concerning hookworm infection is correct except:

options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a correct statement.

options.text: Hookworm infection is acquired by humans when filariiform larvae penetrate the skin
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a correct statement.

options.text: Hookworm infection is caused by Nectar americanus
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a correct statement (though the spelling is Necator).

options.text: Hookworm infection can be diagnosed by finding the trophozoite in the stool
options.feedback: Correct! Hookworm infection is diagnosed by finding eggs, not trophozoites, in the stool.

question: Each of the following statements concerning Trichomonas vaginalis is correct except:

options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a correct statement.

options.text: T. vaginalis can be diagnosed by visualizing the trophozoite
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a correct statement.

options.text: T. vaginalis exists only in the trophozoite stage
options.feedback: Correct! T. vaginalis has no cyst stage.

options.text: T. vaginalis is a flagellated protozoan
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a correct statement.

question: Each of the following statements concerning Giardia lamblia is correct except:

options.text: G. lamblia has both a trophozoite and cyst stage in its life cycle
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a correct statement.

options.text: G. lamblia is transmitted by the fecal oral route from both human and animal sources
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a correct statement.
Correct! Giardia causes malabsorption diarrhea, not hemolytic anemia.

options.text: G. lamblia can be diagnosed by the string test
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a correct statement.

question: Each of the following statements concerning Malaria is correct except

options.text: The female anopheles mosquito is the vector
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a correct statement.
Incorrect. This is a correct statement.

options.text: Release of merozoites from red blood cells cause fever and chills
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a correct statement.

options.text: The principal site of gametocyte formation is the human gastrointestinal tract.
options.feedback: Correct! Gametocytes form in the human blood, not the gastrointestinal tract.

question: Each of the following parasite is transmitted by mosquitoes except:

options.feedback: Correct! Leishmania is transmitted by the sandfly.
Incorrect. W. bancrofti is transmitted by mosquitoes.
Incorrect. P. vivax is transmitted by mosquitoes.
Incorrect. P. falciparum is transmitted by mosquitoes.

question: Pigs or dogs are the source of human infection by each of the following parasites except:

options.feedback: Incorrect. Dogs are the definitive host and a source of infection.
Incorrect. Pigs are the intermediate host and source of infection.
Correct! Ascaris is transmitted from human to human via fecal-oral route.
Incorrect. Pigs are a source of infection for Trichinella.

question: In malaria, the form of plasmodia that is transmitted from mosquito to human is the

options.feedback: Correct! Sporozoites are the infective stage injected by the mosquito.
Incorrect. Gametocytes are the sexual stage taken up by the mosquito.
Incorrect. Merozoites are the asexual stage that infects red blood cells.
Incorrect. Hypnozoites are a dormant liver stage in P. vivax and P. ovale.

question: Trophozoites, Schizonts and gametocytes of all the malarial parasites are seen in the peripheral blood smear except;

options.feedback: Correct! Only ring forms and gametocytes are typically seen in peripheral blood for P. falciparum.
Incorrect. Trophozoites, schizonts, and gametocytes are seen.
Incorrect. Trophozoites, schizonts, and gametocytes are seen.
Incorrect. Trophozoites, schizonts, and gametocytes are seen.

question: Black water fever is a special manifestation of malaria caused by;

options.feedback: Correct! Blackwater fever is a complication of P. falciparum malaria.
Incorrect. This is associated with P. falciparum.
Incorrect. This is associated with P. falciparum.
Incorrect. This is associated with P. falciparum.

question: After sporozoite gain entrance to human body it undergoes developmental cycle first in liver than in RBC, only after which fever is seen. This incubation period varies between plasmodium species, and .... Species has longest incubation period.

options.feedback: Incorrect. P. malariae has the longest incubation period.
Correct! P. malariae has the longest incubation period.
Incorrect. P. malariae has the longest incubation period.
Incorrect. P. malariae has the longest incubation period.

question: Which of the following statement(s) regarding Plasmodium falciparum are true?

options.text: causes more severe disease in pregnancy
options.feedback: Correct! A is true.

options.text: is associated with recurrent relapses after initial treatment because of liver hypnozoites
options.feedback: Incorrect. B is false; P. falciparum does not have a hypnozoite stage.

options.text: is the only malaria parasite causing greater than 20% parasitaemia
options.feedback: Correct! C is true.

options.text: infection is typically associated with thrombocytopenia
options.feedback: Correct! D is true.

options.text: is the only cause of cerebral malaria
options.feedback: Incorrect. E is false; P. vivax can also cause cerebral malaria.

question: Which of the following extracellular enzymes produced by Group A streptococci is called 'spreading factor,' an enzyme important in skin and soft tissue infection?

options.feedback: Incorrect. Streptokinase is a spreading factor but the correct answer is Hyaluronidase.
Correct! Hyaluronidase is known as the 'spreading factor'.
Incorrect. M protein is a virulence factor involved in phagocytosis resistance.
Incorrect. DNase is involved in degrading DNA.
Incorrect. Hyaluronidase is the correct answer.

question: All of the following statements about the M-protein of Group A Streptococci are correct EXCEPT:

options.text: The amino terminal portion (distal portion) is variable, accounting for over 80 distinct serotypes.
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a correct statement.

options.text: M proteins allow streptococci to resist phagocytosis.
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a correct statement.

options.text: Antibodies to M protein confer type-specific immunity.
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a correct statement.

options.text: M protein is the major virulence factor of Group A streptococci.
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a correct statement.

options.text: M protein is the major constituent of the capsule of Group A streptococci.
options.feedback: Correct! The capsule is made of hyaluronic acid.

question: A 12 year old boy presents with acute onset of sore throat, fever to 38.9 degrees C and painful anterior cervical lymphadenopathy. On exam the pharynx is red and swollen and the tonsils are covered with yellow-white exudate. The child also has halitosis. Which of the following nonsuppurative complications are of concern?

options.feedback: Incorrect. Sinusitis is a suppurative complication.
Incorrect. Both ARF and AGN are possible.
Incorrect. Both ARF and AGN are possible.

options.text: Acute rheumatic fever and acute glomerulonephritis
options.feedback: Correct! Both are nonsuppurative complications of GAS pharyngitis.
Incorrect. Scarlet fever is a manifestation of the acute infection, not a complication.

question: Which of the following statements about Group B streptococci (Streptococcus agalactiae) is not correct?

options.text: They are important causes of toxic strep syndrome.
options.feedback: Correct! Toxic shock syndrome is associated with Group A streptococci (Staphylococcus aureus also).

options.text: They are frequent colonizers of the female genital tract.
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a correct statement.

options.text: Screening for this pathogen during pregnancy has reduced the incidence of neonatal sepsis.
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a correct statement.
Incorrect. This is a correct statement.

options.text: They are important causes of urinary tract infections and bacteremia in elderly and diabetic adults.
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a correct statement.

question: Which of the following statements about the 23-valent pneumococcal vaccine is not correct?

options.text: It is a protein-conjugated, polysaccharide vaccine.
options.feedback: Correct! The 23-valent vaccine is a pure polysaccharide vaccine, not protein-conjugated.

options.text: It is poorly immunogenic in young children and immunocompromised hosts.
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a correct statement.

options.text: It is routinely recommended for immune competent adults and children >2 yrs. of age at risk for serious pneumococcal disease.
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a correct statement.

options.text: It protects against the major serotypes of pneumococci causing infection.
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a correct statement.

options.text: An adult with asplenia would be a candidate for this vaccine.
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a correct statement.

question: What is the O antigen of Enterobacteriaceae?

options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a general description.

options.text: A channel controlling substance taken into the organism.
options.feedback: Incorrect. This describes porins.
Incorrect. This describes the H antigen.

options.text: A peptidoglycan matrix important for cellular rigidity
options.feedback: Incorrect. This describes peptidoglycan.
Correct! The O antigen is the polysaccharide portion of LPS.

question: All Enterobacteriaceae share all of the following characteristics EXCEPT:

options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a characteristic of Enterobacteriaceae.
Incorrect. This is a characteristic of Enterobacteriaceae.
Correct! Enterobacteriaceae are oxidase negative.
Incorrect. This is a characteristic of Enterobacteriaceae.
Incorrect. This is a characteristic of Enterobacteriaceae.

question: Which of the following virulence factors of E. coli is important for attachment to host epithelial cells in the pathogenesis of urinary tract infections?

options.feedback: Incorrect. Aerobactin is a siderophore for iron acquisition.
Incorrect. Alpha hemolysin is a toxin.
Incorrect. Urease is more commonly associated with Proteus.
Incorrect. K1 is a polysaccharide capsule.
Correct! Pili (fimbriae) mediate attachment.

question: This urinary pathogen 'swarms' across agar surfaces and may cause bladder and renal calculi (stones).

options.feedback: Incorrect. Citrobacter does not typically swarm.
Incorrect. Enterobacter does not typically swarm.
Incorrect. Serratia does not typically swarm.
Incorrect. Klebsiella does not typically swarm.
Correct! Proteus is known for swarming motility and urease production leading to stones.

question: Which of the following statements regarding Enterotoxigenic E. coli are CORRECT?

options.text: They are important causes of traveler's diarrhea.
options.feedback: Correct! A is correct.

options.text: Transmission occurs from ingestion of contaminated food and water.
options.feedback: Correct! B is correct.

options.text: Disease is caused by production of one or both of two types of enterotoxins.
options.feedback: Correct! C is correct.

options.text: None of the above are correct.
options.feedback: Incorrect. A, B, and C are correct.

question: R factors:

options.text: Are small plasmids which encode resistance to only one type of antibiotic
options.feedback: Incorrect. R factors often encode resistance to multiple antibiotics.

options.text: Contain plasmid elements (replication origins, incompatibility determinants, etc.) that were widespread in the pre-antibiotic era
options.feedback: Correct! R factors are plasmids that existed before the antibiotic era.

options.text: Represent genetically engineered cloning vectors which have escaped into pathogenic bacteria
options.feedback: Incorrect. R factors are naturally occurring.

options.text: All of the above are correct
options.feedback: Incorrect. Only B is correct.

question: Movement of DNA from one bacteria to another through a tubular bridge or pilus:

options.feedback: Correct! Conjugation involves direct cell-to-cell contact via a pilus.
Incorrect. Transposition involves the movement of DNA segments within a genome.
Incorrect. Transfection is the introduction of DNA into eukaryotic cells.
Incorrect. Transduction involves bacteriophages.

question: Which statement describing the potential advantages of DNA technologies over conventional culture-based methods is not true?

options.text: Greater stability of samples during transport
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is generally true.
Incorrect. This is generally true.

options.text: More complete and accurate determination of organism resistance to antibiotics
options.feedback: Correct! DNA tests detect resistance genes, not phenotypic expression, which can be influenced by other factors.
Incorrect. This is generally true.

question: The polymerase chain reaction (PCR):

options.text: Has been adapted for accurate quantification of viruses
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a correct statement (e.g., qPCR).

options.text: May yield false positive results when amplicons contaminate clinical samples
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a correct statement.

options.text: Offers detection sensitivity which often but not always exceeds that of culture
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a correct statement.
Correct! All statements are true.

question: Which of the following is not one of Koch's postulates?

options.text: The organism is regularly found in lesions of the disease
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a Koch's postulate.

options.text: The organism can be isolated from diseased tissues in pure culture on artificial media
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a Koch's postulate.

options.text: Inoculation of this pure culture produces a similar disease in experimental animals
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a Koch's postulate.

options.text: Treatment of the disease with a broad spectrum oral antimicrobial dependably eradicates the organism and cures the disease
options.feedback: Correct! This is not a Koch's postulate.

question: The diagnosis of septicemia should be considered in patients who are at increased risk of blood stream infection (often secondary to local disease, such as urinary tract infection or pneumonia). All of the following are factors predisposing patients to septicemia except:

options.text: Underlying diseases that appear to compromise host defenses, such as diabetes, lymphoma, etc.
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a predisposing factor.

options.text: Patients with a polymorphonuclear leukocyte count less than 1000/mm3
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a predisposing factor (neutropenia).

options.text: Patients with polymorphonuclear leukocyte counts of 10,000 to 20,000
options.feedback: Correct! This is a normal or mildly elevated count and not a predisposing factor in itself.

question: Physical findings and other factors that should suggest the presence of septicemia in a particular patient are all of the following except:

options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a sign of sepsis.
Correct! Conjunctivitis is not a typical sign of septicemia.
Incorrect. These can be signs of sepsis.

options.text: In elderly patients, decreased urine output or mental changes (confusion)
options.feedback: Incorrect. These can be signs of sepsis in the elderly.

question: The most likely organism to be causing septicemia depends, among other things, on the patients' personal risk factors (e.g., their underlying disease), or lab data (e.g. their leukocyte count), 'community risk factors' (such as what particular organisms inhabit the unit on which they happen to be in the hospital); and the physical examination. In a bone marrow transplant patient with a fever of 104 F, who has a white blood cell count of 345mm3, there are scattered skin lesions comprised of a central blue area with an areola of redness (inflammation). You vaguely remember seeing such a lesion before in a lecture. 'Echtyma something or the other' is the best you can do...'Oh yes, with the blue, gangrene-like center, ecthymagangrenosum, that's it' is your mental conversation. In this patient the most likely organism is:

options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a fictional organism.
Correct! Ecthyma gangrenosum is a classic sign of Pseudomonas aeruginosa sepsis in neutropenic patients.
Incorrect. This is a fictional organism.
Incorrect. This is a fictional organism.

question: It is extremely difficult to eradicate staphylococcal infection in the presence of a foreign body. Thus, infected artificial joints and infected intravenous lines often must be removed in conjunction with optimal intravenous anti-staphylococcal therapy with oxacillin or nafcillin (sometimes in combination with rifampin) in order to eradicate such staphylococcal infections (and these attempts are not always successful). Staphylococcal infection of bone may persist for many years despite what should be optimal antimicrobial therapy. The most likely reason for this tenacity of staphylococcal osteomyelitis is:

options.text: The Staphylococcal pilus gene is up-regulated by oxacillin
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is not a recognized mechanism.

options.text: Fragments of dead bone, called 'involucrum' may act as a foreign body, and may have to be removed for optimal eradication of the staphylococcal infection.
options.feedback: Correct! Sequestrum (dead bone) acts as a foreign body.

options.text: Staphylococci may be perceived by the patient's immune system as a positive factor, because the staphylococci produce an extracellular enzyme, obtundokinase, which makes the patient more alert.
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a nonsensical statement.

options.text: Staphylococci are such an integral part of our normal flora that they are treated as 'self' rather than 'non-self', and no immune reaction to them occurs.
options.feedback: Incorrect. S. aureus is not a normal flora in bone.

question: A 42 year old woman who was complaining of shaking chills and fever went to the Salt Lake City Homeless clinic. She had enlarged lymph nodes in the right axilla. 'Upstream' from the enlarged nodes was an ulcer on the top of the patient's right hand. When carefully questioned, the patient said she remembered being bitten there by an insect resembling a horsefly, but with yellow stripes on its abdomen; 'Its what we used to call a deerfly,' she added laconically. The patient denied any contact with rats or fleas. The patient's disease was most likely to be:

options.feedback: Incorrect. Brucellosis is not transmitted by deerfly bites.
Correct! Tularemia can be transmitted by deerfly bites.
Incorrect. Plague is transmitted by fleas or aerosols.
Incorrect. Relapsing fever is transmitted by ticks or lice.

question: A 22 year old female graduate student who works in a day-care center comes to the emergency room where you are on duty. The woman complains of a sore throat, which she has had for two days, and it is not getting any better; further, in the last ten hours, the sore throat has been accompanied by a headache as well as nausea, and vomiting. On physical examination you note a BP of 90/65 and a respiratory rate of 30/min; the temperature is 104 degrees F. The neck is supple but there are tiny red spots distal to the BP cuff. Considering this patient's personal risk factors (her age) and her physical examination, it is clear that the most likely organism to be causing this patient's illness is:

options.feedback: Incorrect. Brucellosis usually presents with undulant fever.
Incorrect. S. aureus pharyngitis is less common.
Correct! Meningococcemia can present with sore throat, fever, and petechiae.
Incorrect. Brucellosis is not typically associated with sore throat or petechiae.

question: A 13 year old boy appears in your emergency room complaining of blurred vision and a dry mouth. His companions say that he has been muttering about a white condor that is going to pick him up and take him to a mountain top. He was a member of a scout troop that has been hiking and camping in the Capitol Reef area of Utah. On physical examination he is afebrile, has a rather ruddy complexion and his pupils are dilated and fixed. He adamantly denies eating any canned food or processed meat, but confesses that on a 'dare' he ate a large trumpet-like white flower that his patrol members brought back from a hike in the desert. The most likely explanation for his illness is:

options.feedback: Incorrect. E. coli O157:H7 causes bloody diarrhea.

options.text: Ingestion of Datura stramonium with a resulting anticholinergic and hallucinogenic reaction
options.feedback: Correct! Datura (Jimsonweed) contains anticholinergic alkaloids causing these symptoms.

options.text: He has a malignant brain tumor
options.feedback: Incorrect. The history points to a toxic ingestion.

options.text: He has been bitten by a brown recluse spider
options.feedback: Incorrect. Brown recluse bites cause necrotic skin lesions.

question: Which of the following diseases are not transmitted by ticks:

options.feedback: Incorrect. Tularemia can be transmitted by ticks.
Correct! Plague is transmitted by fleas.
Incorrect. Relapsing fever can be transmitted by ticks.
Incorrect. Lyme disease is transmitted by ticks.

question: A 20 year old patient comes to the homeless clinic with a skin abscess on the right arm. A swab from the intact skin overlying this abscess grows coagulase negative staphylococci. An aspirate obtained sterilely with a needle and syringe grows Staphylococcus aureus. About these 2 organisms in this patient you can say:

options.text: Both are normal flora and contaminated the specimens sent to the lab
options.feedback: Incorrect. S. aureus from aspirate is significant.

options.text: The staph aureus is a pathogen and the coagulase negative staph is not
options.feedback: Correct! S. aureus is the pathogen; coagulase-negative staph from skin is a contaminant.

options.text: The coagulase negative staphylococcus is a pathogen
options.feedback: Incorrect. In this context, it is a contaminant.

options.text: The coagulase negative staphylococcus is an opportunistic pathogen
options.feedback: Incorrect. It's a contaminant here, though it can be an opportunistic pathogen in other contexts.

question: A person who has a normal healthy immune system acquires a new strain of E. coli that now lives in his gastrointestinal tract. This individual is not ill. A second person with an acute leukemia and no white blood cells acquires the same organism, develops diarrhea followed by hypotension, bacteremia, sepsis, and death. This organism is:

options.feedback: Incorrect. While E. coli is normal flora, it caused disease in the second person.
Incorrect. It did not cause disease in the first person.

options.text: An opportunistic pathogen for both individuals
options.feedback: Incorrect. It did not cause disease in the first person.

options.text: One with a low degree of virulence
options.feedback: Correct! It only caused disease in the severely immunocompromised host.

question: A 25 year old woman has a 4 year old son who is now recovering from Group A strep pharyngitis. Out of curiosity, the pediatrician does a throat culture on the mother which is positive for Group A strep even though she doesn't have a sore throat. The pediatrician also does an ASO titer and anti DNAsE B assay on her both of which are negative. At this point we would say that she:

options.feedback: Incorrect. Asymptomatic infection implies a recent acquisition.
Incorrect. This is not an opportunistic infection.
Correct! Carriers have the organism but no immune response or symptoms.
Incorrect. No evidence of immunosuppression.

question: Characteristics of a bacterial capsule include:

options.feedback: Incorrect. Not all bacteria have a capsule.
Incorrect. Capsules are composed of polysaccharides or polypeptides.

options.text: It is an important mechanism for protecting a bacteria against ingestion by PMNs
options.feedback: Correct! Capsules are anti-phagocytic.

options.text: It is what causes the gram stain reaction
options.feedback: Incorrect. The cell wall determines the Gram stain reaction.

question: As a budding basic scientist you work in a research laboratory and discover a chemical that removes the cell wall from bacteria but leaves the organism undamaged otherwise. Things that the bacteria will lose because of this include:

options.text: The ability to stain gram positive: all will look gram negative
options.feedback: Incorrect. Removing the cell wall would result in no Gram stain result.
Incorrect. Motility is not dependent on the cell wall.

options.text: The ability to have a gram stain result
options.feedback: Correct! The Gram stain depends on the cell wall.

options.text: The ability to attach to other cells
options.feedback: Incorrect. Attachment is not dependent on the cell wall.

question: Under the electron microscope you keep at home next to your MP3 player you look at a single bacterium living in your mouth. You see a single polar flagellum stretching off from one end looking very much like a tail. A characteristic of this organism should be:

options.text: An ability to stay in one place for a very long time
options.feedback: Incorrect. Flagella are for motility.

options.text: An ability to move in the direction opposite the flagellum
options.feedback: Correct! A single polar flagellum propels the bacterium by rotating.

options.text: An ability to avoid ingestion by PMNs
options.feedback: Incorrect. Flagella are not primarily anti-phagocytic.

options.text: An ability to induce a large antibody response
options.feedback: Incorrect. While flagellin is antigenic, the primary characteristic of a flagellum is motility.

question: On the same day the clinical laboratory identifies two strains of the same bacterial species. One of these strains has pili on the surface; the other does not. In terms of the clinical status of the two patients it is likely that:

options.text: The patient with the piliated strain is ill, the other patient is not
options.feedback: Correct! Pili are often virulence factors for attachment.

options.text: The patient with the piliated strain is not ill, the other patient is
options.feedback: Incorrect. Pili are associated with virulence.

options.text: Both patients are ill since pili do not correlate with virulence
options.feedback: Incorrect. Pili often correlate with virulence.

options.text: The patient with the piliated strain is a child, the other patient is an adult
options.feedback: Incorrect. Age is not directly related to pili expression.

question: While you are on your obstetrics rotation you deliver a healthy appearing bouncing baby boy to a 19 year old mother. Three days later the baby becomes lethargic, has a high temperature and vomits. A lumbar puncture shows that the infant has bacterial meningitis. A gram stain of spinal fluid shows short gram positive rods. This organism is:

options.feedback: Correct! Listeria is a short gram-positive rod that causes neonatal meningitis.
Incorrect. Group B strep is a gram-positive coccus.
Incorrect. This is a likely pathogen.
Incorrect. Listeria is facultatively anaerobic.

question: A 30 year old woman is admitted to the hospital with a fever. She was recently diagnosed with acute leukemia and finished a course of chemotherapy last week. As a result of her treatment she now has essentially no white blood cells. As part of her workup you perform 2 blood cultures, a sputum culture, a urine culture, and you obtain a chest x-ray. Of all of these tests, the only positive result is one blood culture growing a gram positive bacillus. This bacillus is:

options.text: The most likely cause of the fever
options.feedback: Incorrect. A single positive culture for a gram-positive bacillus in this context is likely a contaminant.
Correct! Bacillus species are common contaminants.

options.text: An indication that the patient has meningitis
options.feedback: Incorrect. There is no evidence of meningitis.

options.text: Most likely an organism she acquired from the hands of a health care provider the last time she was in the hospital
options.feedback: Incorrect. While possible, it's more likely a contaminant from the skin.

question: You are interested in the microbiology culture results on one of your patients and come to the microbiology laboratory to get an update. The microbiology lab supervisor tells you that your patient is growing gram positive bacilli from both blood cultures you drew and from a urine sample. He volunteers that he doesn't think you ought to worry since these results aren't likely to be important. His rationale is:

options.text: Gram positive bacilli are so rare that they aren't likely to cause disease
options.feedback: Incorrect. Gram-positive bacilli can cause disease (e.g., Listeria, Bacillus anthracis).

options.text: Gram positive bacilli do not cause urinary tract disease
options.feedback: Incorrect. They can, though it's less common.

options.text: Gram positive bacilli are common contaminants so cultures growing them are not of much value
options.feedback: Correct! Gram-positive bacilli are common skin and environmental contaminants.

question: A 30 year old male comes to the hospital complaining of 'stiffness'. He was fine until 2 days ago when he started to have trouble opening his mouth. Later the same day his neck became stiff, and since then he has had increasing trouble with respirations. On exam he has a temperature of 101 degrees, his pulse is 110, his respirations are shallow and at 24/minute. His blood pressure the first time taken by a nurse was 90/50, but now is 120/80. He appears flushed and is sweating profusely. He does appear quite rigid and his face appears to be in a grimace. His reflexes are all hyperactive. You should be worried that this patient has:

options.feedback: Incorrect. Botulism causes flaccid paralysis.
Correct! Trismus (lockjaw), rigidity, and hyperreflexia are classic symptoms.
Incorrect. This presentation is not typical of sepsis.
Incorrect. This is not typical food poisoning.

question: Infections caused by anaerobes are:

options.feedback: Incorrect. They can occur in many sites.

options.text: Never seen in the lung because of its excellent blood supply
options.feedback: Incorrect. Anaerobes can cause lung abscesses.
Correct! Anaerobic infections are often polymicrobial.
Incorrect. They are often chronic or insidious.

question: The laboratory reports that both blood cultures that are drawn from a 15 year old girl are growing Bacteroides fragilis. She was admitted to the hospital because of acute abdominal pain. You should be concerned that:

options.text: She has acute gastroenteritis and the organism is entering the blood from the inflamed bowel endothelial surface
options.feedback: Incorrect. Gastroenteritis is less likely.

options.text: She has a bowel perforation and intraabdominal tissue destruction with impaired vascular flow
options.feedback: Correct! B. fragilis is part of the normal gut flora and bacteremia suggests a breach in the bowel wall.

options.text: She has gram negative sepsis and the Bacteroides is a contaminant
options.feedback: Incorrect. Bacteroides is a known pathogen in this context.

options.text: She has pelvic inflammatory disease with gonorrhea, a sexually transmitted disease, and the Bacteroides is a contaminant
options.feedback: Incorrect. B. fragilis in blood is significant.

question: Anaerobic infections are:

options.feedback: Correct! Anaerobes are part of the normal flora on mucosal surfaces.
Incorrect. They are associated with poor blood supply.
Incorrect. They can be gram-positive or gram-negative.

options.text: Most likely to occur in the lung with its high oxygen level
options.feedback: Incorrect. They occur in areas with low oxygen.

question: Specific organisms that are considered strict anaerobes include:

options.feedback: Incorrect. These are facultative anaerobes.
Incorrect. Klebsiella is facultative.
Correct! Both are strict anaerobes.
Incorrect. Pseudomonas is aerobic; Staphylococcus is facultative.

question: The single most important characteristic of diarrhea caused by Vibrio cholera is:

options.feedback: Correct! Cholera causes massive, watery diarrhea ('rice water stools').
Incorrect. Abdominal pain is not a prominent feature.
Incorrect. Bloody diarrhea is not typical.
Incorrect. Renal insufficiency is a complication, not the primary characteristic.

question: A 15 year old boy comes in to clinic with pain in his right ear. He is on his school swim team and spends about 2 hours per day in the pool. Along with the pain in his ear, he has watery drainage coming from the ear canal. On exam his vital signs are normal, and he appears healthy, but his left external ear looks quite red and there is copious watery, slightly green drainage coming from the ear. The organism mostly likely causing this is:

options.feedback: Incorrect. While possible, Pseudomonas is more likely in swimmers.
Incorrect. These are oral flora.
Incorrect. This is a cause of otitis media, not externa.
Correct! Pseudomonas is associated with 'swimmer's ear' (otitis externa).

question: Helicobacter pylori:

options.text: Is the presumed cause of colon cancer
options.feedback: Incorrect. H. pylori is associated with gastric, not colon, cancer.

options.text: Is the cause of most cases of acute food poisoning in the U.S.
options.feedback: Incorrect. H. pylori is not a food poisoning organism.

options.text: Is the cause of about 90% of peptic ulcers in the U.S.
options.feedback: Correct! H. pylori is the main cause of peptic ulcers.
Incorrect. H. pylori is strongly urease positive.

question: Nonfermentative gram negative bacilli are:

options.feedback: Correct! Nonfermenters are aerobic.
Incorrect. Most are catalase positive.
Incorrect. Hemolysis is variable.
Incorrect. They do not form spores.

question: The normal habitats of nonfermentative bacteria include the following except:

options.feedback: Incorrect. They can contaminate these.
Incorrect. They can be found here.
Incorrect. They can be found in soil/water.
Correct! Reptiles are not a typical habitat for nonfermenters.

question: A 45 year old woman is admitted to the hospital with acute chest pain. On the second hospital day it is clear that she has had a myocardial infarction. Unfortunately, on the same day she develops acute diarrhea. A stool culture grows Salmonella. This infection is:

options.feedback: Incorrect. While acquired in the hospital, the more specific term is 'nosocomial'.
Correct! A nosocomial infection is acquired in the hospital.
Incorrect. Iatrogenic implies a procedure caused it.
Incorrect. It was acquired in the hospital.

question: A common type of nosocomial infection is:

options.feedback: Correct! UTIs, especially catheter-associated, are the most common nosocomial infection.
Incorrect. Meningitis is less common.
Incorrect. Cellulitis is less common.
Incorrect. Gastroenteritis occurs but is less common than UTIs.

question: You receive a urine culture report from the laboratory on a 45 year old patient who is a quadriplegic with an indwelling urinary tract catheter. The urine culture is growing >100,000 oxidase positive gram negative bacilli. The most likely organism is:

options.feedback: Incorrect. E. coli is oxidase negative.
Incorrect. Enterococcus is a gram-positive coccus.
Incorrect. Klebsiella is oxidase negative.
Correct! Pseudomonas is oxidase positive, gram-negative, and a common UTI pathogen in catheterized patients.

question: An important means of preventing hospital acquired infections is:

options.text: For health care workers to wash their hands between patient contacts
options.feedback: Correct! Hand hygiene is the single most important measure.

options.text: For all patients to be placed in single rooms
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is not practical for all patients.

options.text: For all patients to receive a single dose of antibiotics at the time of admission
options.feedback: Incorrect. This would promote resistance.

options.text: An infection control program with one nurse for every 100 patients in the hospital
options.feedback: Incorrect. While infection control is important, this specific ratio is not the primary means.

question: Each of the following statements concerning Q fever is correct EXCEPT:

options.feedback: Correct! Q fever typically presents without a rash.

options.text: It is transmitted by respiratory aerosol
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a correct statement.

options.text: Sheep and cattle are in important reservoir
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a correct statement.

options.text: It is caused by Coxiella burnetii
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a correct statement.

question: Each of the following statements concerning epidemic typhus is correct EXCEPT:

options.text: The disease is characterized by a rash that begins on the trunk and moves distally.
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a correct statement.

options.text: The recrudescent form of the disease is called Brill-Zinsser
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a correct statement.

options.text: The disease is caused by Rickettsia typhi
options.feedback: Correct! Epidemic typhus is caused by Rickettsia prowazekii.

options.text: The causative organism is transmitted to humans by lice
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a correct statement.

question: Which of the following statements is true concerning Q fever:

options.text: The organism is transmitted by fleas
options.feedback: Incorrect. Q fever is transmitted by aerosols.

options.text: Headache, fever, and petechial rash are characteristic features of the disease
options.feedback: Incorrect. Rash is not typical.

options.text: The chronic disease is call Brill-Zinsser
options.feedback: Incorrect. Brill-Zinsser is recrudescent typhus.

options.text: The disease is caused by Coxiella burnetii
options.feedback: Correct! Q fever is caused by Coxiella burnetii.

question: Which of the following is true regarding Rickettsia rickettsii:

options.text: Highest attack rates occur in Colorado and Utah
options.feedback: Incorrect. Highest rates are in the southeastern and south-central US.

options.text: It can be transmitted by flying squirrels
options.feedback: Incorrect. Flying squirrels are associated with R. prowazekii.

options.text: Sheep and cattle are important reservoirs
options.feedback: Incorrect. These are reservoirs for Coxiella.
Correct! R. rickettsii is transmitted by ticks.

question: Which of the following is not transmitted by ticks:

options.feedback: Incorrect. This is transmitted by ticks.
Incorrect. This is transmitted by ticks.
Incorrect. This is transmitted by ticks.
Correct! Q fever is transmitted by aerosols.

question: Which of the following statements concerning Bartonella henselae is correct:

options.feedback: Incorrect. Trench fever is caused by B. quintana.

options.text: Causes Bacillary Angiomatosis in AIDS patients
options.feedback: Correct! B. henselae is a cause of bacillary angiomatosis.
Incorrect. Oroya fever is caused by B. bacilliformis.
Incorrect. Verruga peruana is caused by B. bacilliformis.

question: The lesions of bacillary angiomatosis in AIDS patients are very similar to the hemangiomatous nodules caused by:

options.feedback: Incorrect. R. prowazekii causes typhus.
Incorrect. Ehrlichia causes ehrlichiosis.
Correct! The lesions of verruga peruana (B. bacilliformis) are similar.
Incorrect. Coxiella causes Q fever.

question: Which of the following is transmitted by lice:

options.feedback: Correct! Epidemic typhus is transmitted by lice.
Incorrect. R. typhi is transmitted by fleas.
Incorrect. R. rickettsii is transmitted by ticks.
Incorrect. R. tsutsugamushi is transmitted by mites.

question: A Utah congressman went to Japan as part of a delegation trying to sell the Great Basin National Park to the Japanese so that a Japanese mulching company can convert the 3000-year-old bristlecone pines in the Utah park into mulch for window boxes. About six weeks after some intense entertaining of the American delegation by the Japanese, the congressman develops a low-grade fever and a red-brown macular rash on the palms of his hand and the soles of his feet. He comes to your clinic for evaluation of the rash. He has only vague memory of a tea ceremony involving attractive young Japanese women. Which of the following actions is entirely without a rational basis in this medical situation and/or would not reflect good wisdom on your part:

options.text: You put on gloves to exam the rash further, and wear the gloves to examine the patient's mouth.
options.feedback: Incorrect. Wearing gloves is a good practice, especially if secondary syphilis is suspected.

options.text: You order a serologic test for which the antigen is a non-organism-specific cardiolipin. If the previous test is positive, you may want to confirm it with an organism-specific test like the T. pallidum FTA-ABS test.
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is appropriate for suspected syphilis.

options.text: You tell the patient not to have unprotected sex with anyone until the results of the serologic test in B are available.
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is appropriate to prevent transmission.

options.text: You tell the patient that this rash is most likely the initial manifestation of an infection that is likely to progress quickly, so immediate IV antibiotics are necessary
options.feedback: Correct! This is not rational. The rash of secondary syphilis is not an initial manifestation and does not require immediate IV antibiotics.

question: A 34 year-old geologist who works with an oil-exploration team in Central America comes to your clinic three weeks after returning from a trip to a dry, prairie-dog rich part of Central America. He remembers removing a tick from his leg four days before his departure from Central America, and having his first shaking chill on the plane home. He soon had a series of shaking chills, followed by fever for four days, and before he could get medical care, the fever and chills spontaneously resolved (went away) only to return for four days, then disappear again. His physical exam reveals no rash or organomegaly (enlarged organs such as liver or spleen), but he has a temperature of 104.8o F. As part of the patient's work up, you order a complete blood count. The technician doing the count calls you because there is a long curvaceous bacterium among the red blood cells on the smear of your patient's blood. The most likely cause of your patient's fever is:

options.feedback: Incorrect. Yaws is not tick-borne and does not cause relapsing fever.
Correct! Borrelia species cause relapsing fever with characteristic fever cycles.
Incorrect. Syphilis does not cause relapsing fever.
Incorrect. Pinta is not tick-borne.

question: Which of the following is the metabolically active form of Chlamydia trachomatis?

options.feedback: Incorrect. The elementary body is the infectious, metabolically inactive form.
Incorrect. This is a made-up term.
Incorrect. This is a made-up term.
Correct! The reticulate body is the replicative, metabolically active form.
Incorrect. Reticulate body is the correct answer.

question: Which of the following pathogens is unlikely to be a cause of nongonococcal urethritis?

options.feedback: Incorrect. Ureaplasma is a cause of NGU.
Incorrect. C. trachomatis is the most common cause of NGU.
Incorrect. M. hominis can be associated with urethritis.
Incorrect. M. genitalium is a cause of NGU.
Correct! C. pneumoniae is a respiratory pathogen, not a cause of urethritis.

question: A 24-year-old pigeon fancier presents with fever to 40 degrees C, severe, diffuse headache, malaise, dry hacking cough and muscle aches. Lung exam reveals bilateral crackles and there is hepatosplenomegaly. The most likely pathogen causing this man's infection is:

options.feedback: Incorrect. M. pneumoniae does not typically cause hepatosplenomegaly.
Incorrect. C. pneumoniae does not typically cause hepatosplenomegaly.
Correct! Psittacosis (parrot fever) is associated with exposure to birds and causes fever, cough, and hepatosplenomegaly.
Incorrect. Tularemia can cause pneumonia but is not typically associated with pigeons.
Incorrect. Legionella is not associated with pigeon exposure.

question: Which of the following statements concerning Chlamydia trachomatis infections is NOT correct?

options.text: Serology is the diagnostic test of choice in the diagnosis of acute infection.
options.feedback: Correct! Nucleic acid amplification tests (NAATs) are preferred.

options.text: 50% of infants born to infected mothers during labor develop infections.
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a correct statement.

options.text: Tetracycline's are useful for treatment of C. trachomatis infections.
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a correct statement.

options.text: The clinical spectrum of C. trachomatis sexually transmitted diseases is similar to N. gonorrhoea
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a correct statement.

options.text: Endemic trachoma is a major cause of blindness worldwide.
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a correct statement.

question: Which of the following statements regarding mycoplasmas is NOT correct?

options.text: Mycoplasmas are the smallest free-living micro-organisms.
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a correct statement.

options.text: Mycoplasmas are resistant to cell wall active antibiotics.
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a correct statement.

options.text: Mycoplasmas are common commensals of the respiratory and urogenital tracts.
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a correct statement.

options.text: Mycoplasmas stain well with the Gram's stain.
options.feedback: Correct! Mycoplasmas lack a cell wall and do not stain well.

options.text: Mycoplasmas grow slowly on enriched media.
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a correct statement.

question: Which of the following is the most common clinical syndrome associated with Mycoplasma pneumoniae?

options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a less common complication.
Incorrect. M. pneumoniae causes atypical or walking pneumonia.
Correct! Tracheobronchitis is the most common manifestation.
Incorrect. This is less common.
Incorrect. This is a less common complication.

question: Which of the following pathogens causes invasive joint disease in immuno-compromised hosts?

options.feedback: Incorrect. M. pneumoniae is respiratory.
Correct! M. hominis can cause septic arthritis in immunocompromised patients.
Incorrect. Ureaplasma can cause urethritis.
Incorrect. C. pneumoniae is respiratory.
Incorrect. M. hominis is the correct answer.

question: Which of the following are characteristics that are shared by Mycoplasma hominis and Ureaplasma urealyticum?

options.feedback: Correct! Both can cause postpartum fever.

options.text: Ampicillin would be a reasonable choice for treatment.
options.feedback: Incorrect. Ampicillin targets cell walls, which these organisms lack.

options.text: Both organisms are identified by production of urease.
options.feedback: Incorrect. Urease is a characteristic of Ureaplasma.

options.text: Both stimulate production of cold agglutinins.
options.feedback: Incorrect. Cold agglutinins are associated with M. pneumoniae.

options.text: Both organisms grow well on sheep blood agar
options.feedback: Incorrect. They require specialized media.

question: A 4-year-old child comes to the emergency room with a sore throat, difficulty swallowing, and difficulty breathing. On exam there is obvious difficulty each time the child tries to take in a breath with sounds suggesting obstruction. A lateral neck x-ray shows a swollen epiglottis. The organism most likely to cause this syndrome is:

options.feedback: Correct! Epiglottitis is classically caused by Hib.
Incorrect. S. pneumoniae can cause epiglottitis but is less common.
Incorrect. N. meningitidis can cause epiglottitis but is less common.
Incorrect. S. aureus can cause epiglottitis but is less common.

question: The major virulence factor of Haemophilus influenzae type b is:

options.feedback: Incorrect. Pili are for attachment.
Correct! The polyribitol phosphate (PRP) capsule is the major virulence factor.
Incorrect. The cell wall is not the major virulence factor.
Incorrect. The cell membrane is not the major virulence factor.

question: Important in the laboratory detection of Haemophilus influenzae is that it is:

options.text: Beta hemolytic on sheep blood agar
options.feedback: Incorrect. H. influenzae is not hemolytic.
Incorrect. H. influenzae is catalase positive, but this is not specific.
Incorrect. H. influenzae is facultative.
Correct! H. influenzae requires X and V factors for growth.

question: A common characteristic of Capnocytophaga, Moraxella, and Eikenella is that they:

options.feedback: Incorrect. They are facultative or aerobic.

options.text: Are part of the normal flora of the upper respiratory tract
options.feedback: Correct! These are part of the normal oral flora.
Incorrect. They are not typical UTI pathogens.
Incorrect. They are gram-negative.

question: A common characteristic of Legionella species is that they:

options.feedback: Incorrect. Legionella are gram-negative.
Incorrect. They are not acid-fast.

options.text: Require supplemental ingredients to grow them in laboratory media
options.feedback: Correct! Legionella requires cysteine and iron for growth.
Incorrect. Legionella is intracellular.

question: Most humans become infected with legionella by:

options.feedback: Correct! Legionella is transmitted by aerosolized water.
Incorrect. Legionella is not tick-borne.
Incorrect. Legionella is not mosquito-borne.

options.text: By direct contact with another person who is infected
options.feedback: Incorrect. Legionella is not transmitted person-to-person.

question: Legionella infection is more common in patients who:

options.feedback: Correct! Smokers and those with chronic lung disease are at higher risk.
Incorrect. Diabetes is not a major risk factor.
Incorrect. This is not a typical risk group.

options.text: Are patients on broad spectrum antibiotics
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a risk factor for C. difficile, not Legionella.

question: A 30- year- old patient comes to clinic complaining of a three- week history of cough. He started with a runny nose, fever and sneezing for a few days followed by a dramatic cough that keeps him up at night. He complains of episodes of coughing where he will have 'fits' of coughing with 10- 15 coughs during one exhalation to the point that he is afraid that he will never be able to take in a breath. These coughing episodes are so severe that his ribs are extremely sore. An organism you should consider would be:

options.feedback: Incorrect. S. aureus does not typically cause this presentation.
Incorrect. TB is typically more chronic and indolent.
Correct! Pertussis is characterized by paroxysmal coughing fits.
Incorrect. H. influenzae does not typically cause this presentation.

question: An important characteristic of mycobacteria is that they are:

options.feedback: Incorrect. Mycobacteria are not reliably Gram-stained.
Incorrect. Mycobacteria grow slowly (doubling time ~18 hours).
Correct! Mycobacteria are acid-fast.
Incorrect. This is not a characteristic.

question: You would expect the time course of a typical patient with reactivation tuberculosis before they reach a point requiring hospitalization to take:

options.feedback: Incorrect. TB is a slow, progressive disease.
Incorrect. TB is a slow, progressive disease.
Incorrect. TB is a slow, progressive disease.
Correct! Reactivation TB typically presents with several weeks of symptoms.

question: Symptoms of reactivation tuberculosis include:

options.feedback: Correct! Weight loss is a classic symptom.
Incorrect. Hypotension is not typical.
Incorrect. These are not typical.
Incorrect. Headache is not typical.

question: A 30 year old patient with advanced HIV infection (AIDS) is admitted to the hospital with a high fever. Blood cultures are performed and you receive a report from the laboratory that they are growing a mycobacteria. The one that is most likely is:

options.feedback: Incorrect. M. leprae cannot be cultured in vitro.
Correct! MAC is a common opportunistic infection in AIDS.
Incorrect. M. kansasii can cause disease but is less common than MAC.
Incorrect. M. marinum is associated with fish tanks.

question: An important characteristic of all fungal infections is that:

options.text: They are usually transmitted from person to person
options.feedback: Incorrect. Most fungi are not transmitted person-to-person.

options.text: They are usually simple and straightforward to treat
options.feedback: Incorrect. Fungal infections can be difficult to treat.

options.text: They cause serious disease in debilitated, and/or immunocompromised patients
options.feedback: Correct! Fungi are opportunistic pathogens.
Incorrect. Diagnosis can be challenging.

question: Some fungi have two different morphological forms, yeast and molds. If they do, it is likely that:

options.text: The yeast form appears in the environment, the mold form in tissue
options.feedback: Incorrect. The mold form is in the environment.

options.text: The mold form appears in the environment, the yeast form in tissue
options.feedback: Correct! These are dimorphic fungi.
Incorrect. Typically one form is in tissue.

options.text: Neither form appears in tissue since these are nonpathogenic organisms
options.feedback: Incorrect. Dimorphic fungi are pathogenic.

question: A 30 year old patient with advanced HIV infection comes into clinic complaining that food is sticking in the back of his throat and in his chest when he tries to swallow. You look in his mouth and see patches of whitish material on the surface that can easily be removed and leaves a red base. The organism most likely to cause this is:

options.feedback: Incorrect. Histoplasma causes systemic infection.
Correct! Candidal esophagitis is common in HIV/AIDS.
Incorrect. Strep causes pharyngitis, not this presentation.
Incorrect. Strongyloides is a parasite.

question: The infection most often associated with Cryptococcus neoformans is:

options.feedback: Incorrect. Septic arthritis is not typical.
Incorrect. Sinusitis is not typical.
Incorrect. While pneumonia can occur, meningitis is most common.
Correct! Cryptococcal meningitis is the most common presentation.

question: The usual initiating event when infection occurs with a fungus is for:

options.text: The patient to ingest it from meat
options.feedback: Incorrect. Ingestion is not the typical route.

options.text: The patient to ingest it from water
options.feedback: Incorrect. Water is not the typical route.

options.text: The patient to acquire it from another person
options.feedback: Incorrect. Person-to-person transmission is rare.
Correct! Inhalation of spores is the most common route.

question: A 20 year old college student is part of an archeological team traveling to northeastern Arizona to help excavate Indian ruins. One month after returning she develops a flu-like illness with cough, myalgias, and low grade fever. A chest x-ray shows a small localized infiltrate in the right mid lung field. An organism to consider would be:

options.feedback: Incorrect. Histoplasma is associated with the Ohio/Mississippi River valleys.
Correct! Coccidioidomycosis is endemic in the southwestern US (Arizona).
Incorrect. Legionella is not typically associated with this exposure.
Incorrect. Brucella is not associated with this exposure.

question: A 22 year old diabetic with poor control of blood glucoses comes into clinic with a 2 month history of nasal congestion and low grade fever. Over the past 3 days he has also had a painful right eye-it hurts to look from side to side. In addition he feels that his vision is impaired now in that eye. On exam his right eye appears red and swollen and seems to be pushed forward. His nasal mucosa on that side appears dark and almost necrotic. You should be worried about:

options.feedback: Correct! Mucormycosis is a fungal infection seen in diabetics causing rhinocerebral infection.
Incorrect. TSS is not typically associated with this presentation.
Incorrect. Meningococcemia presents with rash.
Incorrect. Trachoma is a chronic conjunctivitis.

question: The most likely infection to occur following exposure to Sporothrix is:

options.feedback: Incorrect. Sporothrix does not cause UTIs.
Incorrect. Pneumonia is less common.
Incorrect. Meningitis is less common.
Correct! Sporotrichosis causes cutaneous ulcers.

question: Each of the following parasites pass through the human lung at some time during their life cycle but only one establishes a home where it divides and causes a chronic disease state. Which one lives in the lung as a definitive host?

options.feedback: Incorrect. S. stercoralis does not reside permanently in the lung.
Incorrect. W. bancrofti resides in lymphatic vessels.
Incorrect. A. duodenale is a hookworm that resides in the intestine.
Correct! The lung fluke resides in the lung.
Incorrect. A. lumbricoides resides in the intestine.

question: Each of the following is true regarding Pneumocystis carinii. Which statement is false?

options.text: It lives as a commensal in most humans
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a true statement.

options.text: It is a common cause of community acquired pneumonia
options.feedback: Correct! P. carinii is not a common cause of community-acquired pneumonia in immunocompetent hosts.

options.text: It is an AIDS defining illness in patients with HIV
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a true statement.

options.text: It caused pneumonia in malnourished children after World War II
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a true statement.

options.text: The most sensitive way of making the diagnosis is with a direct immunofluorescence stain on sputum or Bronchoalveolar lavage
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a true statement.

question: A 44-year-old native Nigerian who has lived in the USA for 10 years returned to visit his family in Africa and now presents with a history of shaking chills, followed by high fever and then profuse sweating. He states that this is the most severe case of malaria he has ever had. His blood smear shows malaria organisms consistent with Plasmodium falciparum. Which of the following statements is false regarding P. falciparum?

options.text: His symptoms are more severe this time because he has lost some of the immunity that comes with frequent recurrences of malaria
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a true statement.

options.text: Taking appropriate prophylactic medication has an 80 - 90% chance of preventing P. falciparum malaria
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a true statement.

options.text: P. falciparum causes organ damage in the brain, lungs, and kidneys because of adhesion of infected red cells to the endothelium of the capillaries which impairs the microcirculation to these organs
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a true statement.

options.text: Most patients with P. falciparum infection will have a recurrence of infection weeks to months after treatment
options.feedback: Correct! P. falciparum does not have a hypnozoite stage, so relapse does not occur.

question: This July a group of 30 volunteers went to Kenya to help build a school and all of them took prophylaxis to prevent malaria. They claim that they took the medication as prescribed 2 weeks before, during their stay, and for 4 weeks after returning home. In October, one person developed malaria and by April of this year, 9 of the 30 volunteers had been treated for malaria. Which of the following is the least likely explanation for this apparent failure of prevention?

options.text: This is most likely P. vivax or ovale which have a latent stage in their life cycle that persists in the liver and can result in disease weeks to months after exposure
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a plausible explanation.

options.text: Most of the medication used for prophylaxis of malaria acts only on the red blood cell stage of the infection
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a plausible explanation.

options.text: This could be P. malariae which is known to result in a more chronic malaria state
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a plausible explanation.

options.text: This could be P. falciparum that was not completely treated by the prophylaxis
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a plausible explanation.

options.text: The nine who got malaria must not have taken the medication as prescribed since this should have prevented malaria
options.feedback: Correct! This is the least likely explanation, as prophylaxis can fail for other reasons.

question: Each of the following statements (except one) is true regarding malaria. Which one is false?

options.text: Once someone has malaria infection, they will have it for the rest of their life in spite of treatment
options.feedback: Correct! This is false. Only certain species like P. vivax can persist.

options.text: All persons who contract P. vivax malaria have Duffy Antigen present on their red blood cells
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a true statement.

options.text: Persons with sickle cell trait or disease are more resistant to serious malaria
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a true statement.

options.text: Schuffner's dots in red cell cytoplasm are most consistent with P. vivax or ovale malaria
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a true statement.

options.text: One way to help diagnose P. malariae is to carefully record the pattern of symptoms since it is the only species to have a 72 hour interval of symptoms
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a true statement.

question: A 42-year old patient from Ecuador is admitted with fever, right upper abdominal pain and is found to have a cystic mass of the liver. You suspect an amebic abscess and order stool exams that are reported as positive for E. histolytica and an ameba serology is positive at 1:256. Which statement is false regarding ameba?

options.text: He should be treated with two medications. One to kill the active trophozoites and a second to kill the cysts.
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a true statement.
Incorrect. This is a true statement.

options.text: Amebic liver abscess can usually be treated with medication and do not need to be drained
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a true statement.

options.text: Transmission to a new person is generally done by the more resistant cyst form of the life cycle
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a true statement.

options.text: Aspirate from liver cysts can be used to make the diagnosis because they are loaded with white blood cells and E. histolytica cysts
options.feedback: Correct! Amebic abscess fluid typically contains trophozoites, not cysts, and few WBCs.

question: A 30-year old patient is evaluated for chronic diarrhea that has gone on for 4 months with a 20-pound weight loss. By history you find that he has had sex with men for 12 years. You are concerned about the possibility of HIV and his test is positive with a low CD4 count that makes you think of immunodeficiency. You order a test for Cryptosporidium in his stool. Which of the following statements is false regarding Cryptosporidium:

options.text: Most people who have this organism are symptomatic
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a true statement.

options.text: Person to person transmission is common and it is seen frequently in outbreaks in daycare centers
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a true statement.

options.text: It is fairly resistant to chlorine treatment in municipal water treatment centers and is a recognized cause of waterborne outbreaks of diarrhea
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a true statement.

options.text: It is frequently associated with systemic symptoms of fever, chills and bloody diarrhea
options.feedback: Correct! Cryptosporidium typically causes watery diarrhea, not bloody.

options.text: It has been difficult to treat in immunocompromised patients and usually persists for the duration of the patient's life
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a true statement.

question: You see a 20-year old female for complaints of vaginal itching, discharge, pain with intercourse and discomfort with urination. She has had 3 different sexual partners over the past 2 years. As part of your workup, you do a wet mount of vaginal secretions and see a flagellated organism about the size of a white blood cell with 'twitching motility' that makes you think of Trichomonas vaginalis. Which statement is false regarding Trichomonas?

options.text: Men are most likely asymptomatic but efficient transmitters of this organism
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a true statement.

options.text: The cyst form of the life cycle is the most common method of transmission
options.feedback: Correct! T. vaginalis does not have a cyst form.

options.text: Simultaneous treatment of the patient and her sexual contacts will decrease the chance of recurrences
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a true statement.

options.text: In severe cases the regional mucosa may be red with petechial lesions and the cervix has been described as a 'strawberry cervix'
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a true statement.

options.text: Patients found to have Trichomonas should be evaluated for other venereal transmitted diseases
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a true statement.

question: A 22-year old college student is seen because he has had severe chronic diarrhea and a stool for O&P is positive for Giardia lamblia. He has lost 50 pounds in weight and has been treated for Giardia on 6 occasions only to have it reoccur. You are concerned that he has an immunodeficiency and lab results demonstrate a very low IgA level. Which of the following statements would be false regarding Giardia?

options.text: It is one of the causes of H2S or rotten egg gas
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a true statement.

options.text: It is often a self-limiting disease
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a true statement.

options.text: A weight loss of 50 pounds due to Giardia is very unusual
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a true statement.

options.text: You are not surprised when 5 of 11 dorm mates are found to have Giardia because it is spread from person to person easily
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a true statement.

options.text: It often results in iron deficiency anemia due to gastrointestinal blood loss
options.feedback: Correct! Giardia causes malabsorption, not blood loss.

question: This year there have been 7 travelers to eastern Africa that have contracted Trypanosoma brucei rhodesiense. They have all presented in the early stage of the disease with a 'chancre' of the skin associated with cellulitis (red hot swelling) around the chancre. Which of the following is false regarding Trypanosoma brucei?

options.text: Progression of the disease is more rapid in rhodesiense than in gambiense
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a true statement.

options.text: Early diagnosis allows for a more effective, safer medication that can be used before the parasite reaches the brain
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a true statement.

options.text: Rhodesiense has multiple hosts while in gambiense, humans are the only host
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a true statement.

options.text: Epidemics of African sleeping sickness have occurred by person to person spread
options.feedback: Correct! It is transmitted by the tsetse fly, not person-to-person.

options.text: Both are transmitted by the Tsetse fly
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a true statement.

question: Blood banks in Southern California and Texas have done studies to evaluate how often blood containing Trypanosoma cruzi is found in their blood donor pool. They have found that the incidence varies between 1:8,000 to 1:40,000 donors. If you were making up a list of questions to ask blood donors, which of the following questions would be least helpful in screening to exclude this organism from persons that have grown up in an endemic area?

options.text: Have you ever been bitten by a reduvid bug?
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a relevant question.

options.text: Have you ever eaten pigs, dogs, or other animals that are reservoir hosts for Chaga's disease?
options.feedback: Correct! Transmission is not through eating animals.

options.text: Have you lived in an adobe hut with a thatched roof?
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a relevant question.

options.text: Have you ever had swelling of your eye that lasted more than 3 days?
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a relevant question (Romaña's sign).

options.text: Have you ever had a blood test positive for Chaga's disease?
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a relevant question.

question: This year a report from India indicates that a single dose of Amphotericin B can result in the cure of Leishmania donovani rather than prolonged treatment by fairly toxic medication. If confirmed this will be a major advance in the treatment of Kala-azar. Which of the following statements is false regarding systemic Leishmaniasis?

options.text: It is spread by the bite of the sand fly
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a true statement.

options.text: Macrophages ingest the organism and move about the body facilitating its spread
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a true statement.

options.text: Diagnosis can often be made by identifying amastigotes in cells aspirated from the spleen or bone marrow
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a true statement.

options.text: Untreated patients frequently recover on their own
options.feedback: Correct! Visceral leishmaniasis is fatal if untreated.

options.text: Kala-azar should be considered as one of the possibilities in someone who has lived in an endemic area and has an enlarged spleen.
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a true statement.

question: You have just examined a patient from Brazil who has a chronic ulcer of his ear that has been present for 6 months. You are concerned that he has Leishmaniasis and want to differentiate L. mexicana from L. braziliensis. Which of the following statements about these 2 organisms is false?

options.text: They are both transmitted by the sand fly
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a true statement.

options.text: Both appear as amastigotes in macrophages on biopsy
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a true statement.

options.text: Both can start as a cutaneous ulcer that eventually heals on its own
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a true statement.

options.text: Both heal with a residual scar
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a true statement.

options.text: Both will eventually cause lesions of the mucous membranes
options.feedback: Correct! L. braziliensis can cause mucosal leishmaniasis, but L. mexicana does not.

question: Several years ago there was an outbreak of Trichinosis among a group of Vietnam immigrants in New York that had made their own pork sausage. About 30 people who ate the sausage all become ill. Which of the following statements is false about Trichinella spiralis?

options.text: It can be spread by eggs passed in the stool
options.feedback: Correct! Trichinella is transmitted by ingestion of larvae in meat, not eggs.

options.text: It is a tissue nematode that induces a high eosinophil response
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a true statement.

options.text: It is a disease of meat eating animals
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a true statement.
Incorrect. This is a true statement.

options.text: Heavy infection causes symptoms of diarrhea and abdominal pain that progresses to fever, facial and periorbital edema and myalgias
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a true statement.

question: You see a young child for evaluation of fever, abdominal pain, enlarged liver and eosinophilia. You suspect toxocariasis from Toxocara canis or cati. Which of the following statements is false about Toxocara?

options.text: It can result in retinal lesions that can impair vision
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a true statement.

options.text: It is caused by the larvae of the 'dog or cat ascaris' that wander through the body unable to find the lung to complete their life cycle
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a true statement.

options.text: It is associated with persons who eat dirt
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a true statement.

options.text: It can be spread by contact with human feces
options.feedback: Correct! Toxocara is spread by animal feces, not human.

options.text: It is often found in young puppies or kittens
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a true statement.

question: A 4th year medical student from the UofU traveled to Northern Ghana during an international rotation to Accra. He was fortunate enough to see a patient with Dracunculus medinensis in which they were extracting a worm from his leg onto a pencil. This parasite is being eradicated from the world. Which of the following is least likely to help rid the world of Dracunculus medinensis?

options.text: Boiling surface water before drinking it
options.feedback: Incorrect. This would help.

options.text: Using a coffee filter or shirt to filter out copepods before drinking surface water
options.feedback: Incorrect. This would help.

options.text: Treating of sewage before it enters fresh surface water
options.feedback: Correct! Dracunculus is not transmitted through sewage.

options.text: Preventing persons suspected of having Dracunculus from wading in the drinking water
options.feedback: Incorrect. This would help.

options.text: Treating ponds used for drinking water with insecticide
options.feedback: Incorrect. This would help.

question: A 74-year old World War II Veteran who was a prisoner of war in the Philippines is admitted to the VAH with a Group A Streptococcal cellulitis resulting from massive swelling of both lower legs from elephantiasis. Which of the following is false regarding elephantiasis caused by Wuchereria bancrofti?

options.text: If these infections are not treated early they rapidly progress to massive swelling of the involved lymphatic drainage area
options.feedback: Correct! Elephantiasis is a late, chronic complication that develops slowly.

options.text: It is transmitted to humans by the mosquito
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a true statement.
Incorrect. This is a true statement.

options.text: The microfilaria in the blood stream of man can be transfused to another person without establishing a new infection
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a true statement.

options.text: The adult organisms live in the lymphatic channels of man and can result in scarring of the Lymphatic channels
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a true statement.

question: An ophthalmologist from Cameroon, Africa describes the microfilaria seen in the eye of patients with Onchocerciasis. He states that he treats only after finding 30+ microfilaria because of the toxicity of Diethyl-Carbamazine that was used for treatment. Now with a less toxic agent, (Ivermectin) a major attempt is being made to eradicate Onchocerca volvulus. Which statement is false regarding Onchocerca volvulus?

options.text: It is transmitted by the Simulian Black fly that reproduces in rapidly moving water
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a true statement.

options.text: Itching of the skin is a prominent feature of this disease
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a true statement.

options.text: The adults live in a nodule on the skin with the female producing large numbers of microfilaria that migrate under the skin
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a true statement.

options.text: Diagnosis can sometimes be made by finding the microfilaria in a skin snip or skin biopsy
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a true statement.

options.text: The microfilaria mate in the human skin and develop into the adult parasite
options.feedback: Correct! Adults mate, but microfilariae are produced by adults; they do not develop from microfilariae.

question: Itching around the rectum is commonly caused by the pinworm, Enterobius vermicularis. Which of the following is false regarding pinworms?

options.text: It has a simple rectum to mouth life cycle (do not scratch your bum and suck your thumb)
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a true statement.

options.text: Diagnosis is best made by a stool O&P exam
options.feedback: Correct! Diagnosis is best made by the Scotch tape test, not stool O&P.

options.text: It is highly contagious infecting a number of members of a family at the same time
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a true statement.

options.text: Spread can be prevented by good hygiene
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a true statement.

options.text: The egg is oval, flat on one side and contains a single larvae folded over like a safety pin
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a true statement.

question: Trichuris trichiura or whipworm is a frequent parasite found in humans. Which of the following is false for Trichuris trichiura?
